text: preencoded.png Exploring Word Formation Processes in English English, a dynamic and evolving language, constantly adapts and expands its vocabulary through various word formation processes. These processes allow new words to enter the language, reflecting cultural, technological, and social changes. This presentation will delve into the fascinating ways words are created, adapted, and integrated into the English lexicon, providing a comprehensive overview of these linguistic mechanisms. by Sneha
Sarkar

preencoded.png Coinage: Creating New Words from Scratch Coinage, also known as invention, involves creating entirely new words that have no connection to existing words. These neolo gisms often emerge from specific contexts, such as brand names becoming generic terms (e.g., "Kleenex" for facial tissue) or technological innovations introducing new terms (e.g., "internet"). Coined words can fill lexical gaps and reflect emerging c oncepts or products. Xerox is one of the most recognizable examples

of Coinage. Originally a brand name for a photocopying machine, it has entered our everyday language and become a synonym for 'photocopy'. Aspirin is another, originally a brand name, is now universally used to refer to the pain -relieving medication. Innovation Coinage often stems from new inventions or ideas, requiring a novel term to describe them. Branding Successful brand names can become generic terms through widespread usage. Uniqueness Coinage fills lexical gaps by providing words for previously
unnamed concepts.

preencoded.png Borrowing: Adopting Words from Other Languages Borrowing, or loaning, is the process of adopting words from other languages. Throughout its history, English has borrowed extensively from various languages, including Latin, Greek, French, and many others. These borrowed words enrich the English vocabulary and reflect historical and cultural interactions. For example, the word "croissant" comes from French, "kindergarten" comes from German, and "sushi" comes from Japanese. Each borrowed

word carries with it a piece of its origin culture, enriching the English language. Cultural Exchange Borrowing reflects the exchange of ideas, goods, and customs between cultures.Linguistic Evolution Borrowing contributes to the continuous evolution and diversification of language.

preencoded.png Compounding: Combining Existing Words Compounding involves combining two or more existing words to create a new word with a distinct meaning. These compounds can be written as one word (e.g., "sunflower"), two words (e.g., "ice cream"), or hyphenated words (e.g., "well -being"). Compounding is a productive way to expand vocabulary by creating new terms from familiar elements. Some examples of compound words include "keyboard" (key + board), "firefighter" (fire + fighter), and "waterfall"

(water + fa ll). Compound words can often succinctly describe concepts that would otherwise require longer phrases. Simplicity Compounding simplifies complex ideas into single, easily understood words. Efficiency New words are formed by combining familiar elements, promoting quicker understanding. Clarity Compound words can succinctly describe concepts that would otherwise require longer phrases.

preencoded.png Derivation: Adding Affixes to Change Meaning Derivation involves adding prefixes or suffixes (affixes) to existing words to create new words with altered meanings or gram matical functions. Prefixes are added to the beginning of a word (e.g., "un -" in "unhappy"), while suffixes are added to the end (e.g., " -ness" in "happines s"). Derivation is a common and versatile word formation process. Common examples of prefixes include "pre -" (preview), "re -" (rewrite), and "dis -" (disagree).

Common suffixes include " -able" (r eadable), " -er" (writer), and "-ly" (quickly). Derivation allows for nuanced expression and grammatical flexibility. Prefixes Alter the meaning of a word when added to the beginning. 1Suffixes Change the meaning or grammatical function when added to the end. 2 Affixes Enable nuanced expression and grammatical flexibility in language. 3

preencoded.png Conversion: Shifting Word Class Without Affixes Conversion, also known as zero derivation, involves changing the grammatical function of a word without adding any affixes. F or example, a noun can become a verb (e.g., "email" as both a noun and a verb) or an adjective can become a noun (e.g., "the poo r"). Conversion is an economical way to expand vocabulary by repurposing existing words. Other examples of conversion include "text" (noun and verb), "run" (noun and verb), and "empty"

(adjective and verb). Convers ion demonstrates the flexibility and adaptability of the English language. 1 Flexibility Conversion showcases the adaptability of words to serve multiple grammatical roles.2 Economy It expands vocabulary efficiently by repurposing existing words without additional affixes.3 Versatility Words readily shift between noun, verb, and adjective functions based on context.

preencoded.png Acronyms and Initialisms: Shortening Phrases Acronyms and initialisms involve shortening phrases by using the initial letters of each word. Acronyms are pronounced as wor ds (e.g., "NASA"), while initialisms are pronounced letter by letter (e.g., "FBI"). These shortenings provide concise and convenient ways to refer to complex enti ties or concepts. Other examples of acronyms include "laser" (light amplification by stimulated emission of radiation) and "radar" (radio detec tion and

ranging). Initialisms include "ATM" (automated teller machine) and "CEO" (chief executive officer). Acronyms and initialisms are prevalent in technical, go vernmental, and business contexts. NASA Space Agency An acronym representing the National Aeronautics and Space Administration.FBI Law Enforcement An initialism for the Federal Bureau of Investigation.

preencoded.png Blending: Fusing Parts of Two Words Blending involves combining parts of two or more words to create a new word. Typically, the beginning of one word is combined with the end of another (e.g., "smog" from "smoke" and "fog"). Blending creates concise and often playful words that capture elements of both source words. Common examples of blends include "brunch" (breakfast + lunch), "motel" (motor + hotel), and "electrocute" (electric + execut e). Blending is a creative and efficient

word formation process that adds novelty and expressiveness to the language. 1Conciseness Blends create new words that are shorter than the phrases they represent. 2Creativity Blending allows for playful and imaginative word formation. 3Efficiency Combines elements of both source words to create novel meanings.

GATE/CS/DSA/SLP/Ch.1_Notes/Pg.1 Topic 1 : Introduction to Data Structures INTRODUCTION A computer is a machine that processes information and data. The study of data structures includes how this information and data is related and how to use this data efficiently for various applications. Logical properties of a data type are specified by abstract data type, or ADT. x A data type consists of: i) Values (value definition) and ii) Set of operations on these values (Operator definition). x These values

and the operations on them form a mathematical construct that can be implemented using hardware or software data structures. Note : ADT is not concerned with implementation details. x Any two values in an ADT are equal if and only if the values of their components are equal. Operator Definition Header Pre-conditions Post-conditions Value Definition Definition Clause Condition Clause

Vidyalankar : GATE – CS GATE/CS/DSA/SLP/Ch.1_Notes/Pg.2 LINKED LISTS x There are certain drawbacks of using sequential storage to represent arrays. i) A fixed amount of storage remains allocated to the array even when the structure is actually using a smaller amount or possibly no storage at all. ii) Only fixed amount of storage may be allocated, making overflow a possibility. x In a sequential representation, the items of an array are implicitly ordered by the sequential order of storage. If arr[x]

represents an element of an array, the next element will be arr[x + 1] x Suppose that the items of an array were explicitly ordered i.e. each item contained within itself the address of the next element. Such an explicit ordering gives rise to a data structure known as a Linear linked list as shown in the figure below. o Each item in the list is called a node and it contains two fields. i) Information field: It holds the actual element on the list. ii) The next address field: It contains the address

of the next node in the list. Such an address, which is used to access a particular node, is known as a pointer . o The entire linked list is accessed from an external pointer list that points to (contains the address of) the first node in the list. o The next address field of the last node in the list contains a special value called null. This is not a valid address and is used to signal the end of a list. o The list with no nodes on it is called the empty list or the null list. The value of the

external pointer list to such a list is the null pointer. A list can be initialized to the empty list by the operation list = null. INSERTING AND REMOVING NODES FROM A LIST Insertion A list is a dynamic data structure. The number of nodes on a list may vary as elements are inserted and removed. The dynamic nature of a list may be contrasted with the static nature of an array, whose size remains constant. Fig.1: Linear linked list

Notes on Introduction to Data Structures GATE/CS/DSA/SLP/Ch.1_Notes/Pg.3 Example : Suppose that we are given a list of integers as illustrated in figure 2(a) and we have to add the integer 6 to the front of that list i.e. we want the list in figure 2(f). o Assume the existence of mechanism for obtaining empty nodes. The operation p = getnode( ); obtains an empty node and sets the contents of a variable named p to the address of that node. The value of p is then a pointer to this newly allocated

node. o In figure 2(b), we see the list and the new node after performing the getnode operation. The next step is to insert the integer 6 into the info portion of the newly allocated node. This is done by info (p) = 6; (The result is shown in figure 2(c)) o After setting the info portion of node (p), it is necessary to set the next portion of that node. Since node (p) is to be inserted at the front of the list, the node that follows should be the current first node on the list. Since the variable

list contains the address of that first node, node (p) can be added to the list by performing the operation next (p) = list; [This operation places the value of list (which is the address of first node on the list) into the next field of node(p)] Fig.2: Adding an element to the front of a list

Vidyalankar : GATE – CS GATE/CS/DSA/SLP/Ch.1_Notes/Pg.4 o At this point, p points to the list with the additional item included. However, since list is the external pointer to the desired list, its value must be modified to the address of the new first node of the list. This can be done by performing the operation list = p; which changes the value of list to the value of p. figure 2(e) illustrates the result of this operation. o Generalized Algorithm p = getnode( ) [allocate memory for new node]

info(p) = x; next(p) = list ; list = p; Deletion The following figure shows the process of removing the first node of a nonempty list and storing the value of its info field into a variable x. The initial step and final step are shown in figure 3(a) and 3(f) respectively. Fig.3: Removing a node from the front of a list

Notes on Introduction to Data Structures GATE/CS/DSA/SLP/Ch.1_Notes/Pg.5 (Note: This process is exactly opposite to the process of adding a node) o To obtain figure 3(d) from figure 3(a) the following operations are performed. p = list; list = next(p); x = info(p); o In figure 3(d), p still points to the node that was formally first on the list. That node is currently useless because it is no longer on the list and its information has been stored in x. (Note : The node is not considered to be on

the list despite the fact that next(p) points to a node on the list, since there is no way to reach node(p) from the external pointer list) o During the process of removing the first node from the list, the variable p is used as an auxillary variable. The starting and ending configurations of the list make no reference to p. But once the value of p is changed there is no way to access the node at all, since neither an external pointer nor a next field contains its address. Therefore the node is

currently useless and cannot be reused. x The getnode creates a new node, whereas freenode destroys a node. Figure 3(e) and 3(f) depicts a node being freed. STACK A Stack is an ordered collection of items into which new items may be inserted or deleted only at one end, called the top of the stack. x A stack is different from an array as it has the provision for the insertion and deletions of items, thus a stack is a dynamic, constantly changing object. ³ E D C B A Top Elements can be added or deleted
from here Current top element of the stack is E Fig.4: Stack containing stack terms

Vidyalankar : GATE – CS GATE/CS/DSA/SLP/Ch.1_Notes/Pg.6 x The last element inserted into a stack is the first element deleted. Thus stack is called a lastin, Firstout (or LIFO) list. x One cannot distinguish between frame (a) and frame (e) by looking at the stack’s state at the two instances. No record is kept on the stack of the fact that two items had been pushed and popped in the meantime. x The true picture of a stack is given by a view from the top looking down, rather than from a side looking

in. Thus there is no perceptible difference between frame (e) and (d) One can compare the stacks at the two frames by removing all the elements on both stacks and compare them individually. OPERATIONS ON STACK When an item is added to a stack, it is pushed onto the stack. When an item is removed, it is popped from the stack. x Given a stack s and an item i, performing the operation push (s, i) adds item i to the top of stack s. x The operation i = pop(s); removes the element at the top of s and

assigns its value to i. As a push operation adds elements onto a stack, a stack is sometimes called a pushdown list. x There is no upper limit on the number of items that may be kept in a stack, though a pop operation cannot be applied to the empty stack as such a stack has no elements to pop. ³ ³E D C B A G F E D C B A E D C B A D C B A C B A E C B A F E D C B A F E D C B A (a) (b) (c) (d) (e) (f) (g) (h) Fig.5: Motion picture of a stack Top

Notes on Introduction to Data Structures GATE/CS/DSA/SLP/Ch.1_Notes/Pg.7 x The operation empty(s) determines whether stack s is empty or not. If the stack is empty; empty(s) returns the value TRUE, otherwise it will return the value FALSE. x The operation stacktop(s) returns the top element of stack s. The stacktop(s) operation can be decomposed into a pop and a push. It is also called as peek operation. i = s t a c k t o p ( s ) ; is equivalent to i = pop(s); push (s, i); stacktop is not defined

for an empty stack. The result of an illegal attempt to pop or access an item from an empty stack is called underflow. Operation Function Push(s, i) Adds item i on the top of stack s pop(s) Removes the top element of stack s empty(s) Determines whether or not stack s is empty stacktop(s) Returns the top element of stack s without popping it APPLICATIONS OF STACKS The place where stacks are frequently used is in evaluation of arithmetic expression. An arithmetic expression consists of operands and

operators. The operands can be numeric values or numeric variables. The operators used in an arithmetic expression represent the operations like addition, subtraction, multiplication, division and exponentiation. If the operator is situated in between the operands, the representation is called infix. If the operator precedes the operands, the representation is called prefix. If the operator follows the operands, the representation is called postfix. To fix the order of evaluation of an expression

each language assigns to each operator a priority. Even after assigning priorities how can a compiler accept an expression and produce correct code? A polish mathematician Jan Lukasiewicz suggested a notation called Polish notation, which gives two alternatives to represent an arithmetic expression. The notations are prefix and postfix notations. The fundamental property of Polish notation is that the order in which the operation are to be performed is completely determined by the positions of the
operators and operands in the expression. Hence, parentheses are not required while writing expressions in Polish notation. ³ ³

Vidyalankar : GATE – CS GATE/CS/DSA/SLP/Ch.1_Notes/Pg.8 While writing an arithmetic expression, the operator symbol is usually placed between two operands. Abstract Data Types A useful tool for specifying the logical properties of a data type is the abstract data type or ADT. Fundamentally, a data type is a collection of values and a set of operations on those values. The collection and the operations form a mathematical construct that may be implemented using a particular hardware or software data structure. The term "abstract data type" refers to the

basic mathematical concept that defines the data type. In defining an abstract data type as a mathematical concept, we are not concerned with space or time efficiency. Those are implementation details. It is not necessary to implement a particular ADT on a particular piece of hardware or using a particular software system. The time complexity of an algorithm is the amount of computer time it needs to run to completion. x Performance evaluation can be loosely divided into two major phases. 1) A priori estimates (performance analysis) 2) A posteriori testing (performance measurement) x Space complexity Consider the following 3 algorithms:

the instruction space i.e. space for code. o Space for simple variables, fixed size component variables and space for constants and so on. ii) A variable part that consists of the space needed by component variables whose size is dependent on the particular problem instance being solved. o It includes space needed by referenced variables. o Recursion stack space. The space required S(P) of any algorithm P can therefore be written as S(P) = C + S P (instance characteristics) where C is a constant.

time. The compile time does not depend on the instance characteristics. This runtime is denoted by Tp (instance characteristics). To obtain such time at instance, we need to know how many computations i.e. additions, multiplications, subtractions, divisions performed by an algorithm. But still obtaining a correct formula for each algorithm is an impossible task, since the time needed for an addition, subtraction, multiplication and so on, often depends on the numbers being added, subtracted, multiplied

and so on. The value of Tp(n) for any given input size n can be obtained only experimentally. The program is typed, compiled and run on a particular machine. The execution time is physically clocked and Tp(n) is obtained. We can determine the number of steps needed by a program to solve a particular problem instance in one of two ways. i) In the first method, we introduce a new variable, count, into the program. This is a global variable with initial value 0. Statements to increment count by the

appropriate amount are introduced into the program. This is done so that each time a statement in the original program is executed; count is incremented by the step count of that statement. ii) The second method to determine the step count of an algorithm is to build a table in which we list the total number of steps contributed by each statement. This number is often arrived at, by first determining the number of steps per execution (s/e) of the statement and the total number of times (i.e. frequency)

each statement is executed. The s/e of a statement is the amount by which the count changes as a result of the execution of that statement. By combining these two quantities the total contribution of each statement is obtained. By adding the contributions of all statements, the step count for entire algorithm is obtained. It is important, however, to understand the precise meaning of the notation so that when it

The order of growth of the running time of an algorithm, gives a simple characterization of the algorithm’s efficiency and also allows us to compare the relative performance of alternative algorithms. For large enough inputs, the multiplicative constants and lowerorder terms of an exact running time are dominated by the effects

of the input size itself. When we look at input sizes large enough to make only the order of growth of the running time relevant, we are studying the asymptotic efficiency of algorithms. That is, we are concerned with how the running time of an algorithm increases with the size of the input in the limit, as the size of the input increases without bound. Usually, an algorithm that is asymptotically more efficient will be the best choice for all but very small inputs. Comparison of Functions Many

Notes on Introduction to Data Structures GATE/CS/DSA/SLP/Ch.1_Notes/Pg.37 LMR (LAST MINUTE REVISION) x A useful tool for specifying the logical properties of a data type is the abstract data type or ADT. A data type is a collection of values and a set of operations on those values. The term “abstract data type” refers to a basic mathematical concept that defines the data type. x A stack is an ordered collection of items into which new items may be inserted and from which items may be deleted at

one end called the top of the stack. A stack is also called a LIFO list. x Various primitive operations perform on stacks: 1. Push: inserting an element on stack. 2. Pop: deleting an element from the stack. x Since the push operation is what adds elements to a stack, a stack is sometimes called a pushdown list. If a stack contains a single item and the stack is popped, the resulting stack contains no items and is called an empty stack. x The nesting depth at a particular point in an expression is

the number of scopes (left parentheses) that have been opened but not yet closed at that point. This is the same as the number of left parenthesis encountered whose matching right parenthesis have not yet been encountered. x The parentheses count at a particular point in an expression as the number of left parentheses minus the number of right parentheses that have been encountered in scanning the expression from its left end upto that particular point. If the parentheses count is non negative,

it is the same as the nesting depth. x An expression can be represented in either of the following formats: i) infix : A + B ii) postfix : AB+ iii) prefix : +AB where A and B are operands and + is an operator and all these are interconvertable with various operator precedence rules. x A queue is an ordered collection of items from which elements may be deleted at one end (called the front of the queue) and into which items may be inserted at the other end called the rear of the queue.) The result

of an illegal attempt to remove an element from an empty queue is called underflow. x A priority queue is a data structure in which the intrinsic ordering of the elements determines the results of its basic operations. There are two types: i) Ascending priority queue: is a collection of items into which items can be inserted arbitrarily and from which only the smallest item can be removed. ii) Descending priority queue: is similar but allows deletion of only the largest item. To avoid above drawbacks, use lists to implement stacks and queues. Linked List x Drawbacks

of using sequential storage to represent stacks and queues : (i) A fixed amount of storage remains allocated to the stack or queue even when the structure is actually using a smaller amount or possibly no storage at all. (ii) Only fixed amount of storage may be allocated so overflow is possible. x Suppose that the items of a stack or a queue were explicitly ordered i.e. each item contained within itself the address of the next item. Such an explicit ordering gives rise to a data structure known

as a Linear Link list. x A list is a dynamic data structure. The number of nodes on a list may vary dramatically as elements are inserted and removed. o Each item in the list is called a node and it contains two fields: (i) Information field: It holds the actual element on the list. (ii) The next address field: It contains the address of the next node in the list. Such an address, which is used to access a particular node, is known as a pointer. o The entire linked list is accessed from an external

pointer list that points to (contains the address of) the first node in the list. o The next address field of the last node in the list contains a special value called as null. This is not a valid address and used to signal the end of a list. o The list with no nodes on it is called the empty list or the null list. The value of external pointer list to such a list is the null pointer. A list can be initialized to the empty list by the operation; list:= null. Inserting and Removing Nodes from a List

x A list is a dynamic data structure. The number of nodes on a list may vary as elements are inserted and removed. The dynamic nature of a list may be contrasted with the static nature of an array, whose size remains constant. Linked implementation of Stacks x The operation of adding an element to the front of a linked list is similar to that of pushing an element onto a stack. A new item is added as the only immediately accessible item in a collection in both cases. x A stack may be represented

by a Linear linked list. The first node of the list is the top of the stack. o Advantages: i) All stacks being used by a program can share the same available list of nodes and when any stack needs a node, it can obtain it from the single available list.

Notes on Introduction to Data Structures GATE/CS/DSA/SLP/Ch.1_Notes/Pg.39 ii) As long as the total amount of space needed by all the stacks at any one time is less than the amount of space initially available to them, each stack is allowed to grow and shrink to any size. iii) No space has been pre-allocated to any single stack and no stack is using space that it does not need. Linked implementation of Queues: x Linked Lists are not only used for implementing stacks and queues but also as data structures.

An item is accessed in a linked list by traversing the list from its beginning. A list implementation requires n operations to access the n th item in a group but an array requires only a single operation. x A priority queue implemented as an ordered linked list requires examining an average of approximately n/2 nodes for insertion but only one node for deletion. x Advantages of a list over an array for implementing a priority queue. i) No shifting of elements or gaps is necessary in a list. ii)

An item can be inserted into a list without moving any other items, whereas this is impossible for an array unless extra space is left empty. x Performance Analysis: The space complexity of an algorithm is the amount of memory it needs to run to completion. The time complexity of an algorithm is the amount of computer time it needs to run to completion x Performance evaluation can be loosely divided into two major phases. 1) A priori estimates (performance analysis) 2) A posteriori testing (performance

measurement) x Time Complexity: The time T(p) taken by a program p is the sum of the compile time and the run (or execution) time. The compile time does not depend on the instance characteristics. This runtime is denoted by Tp (instance characteristics). x Asymptotic Notation (O, :, T) 1) Big oh (O) The function f(n) = O(g(n)) (read as ‘f of n is big oh of g of n’) iff there exist positive constants C and n 0, such that f(n) d C * g(n) for all n, n t n0. 2) Omega ( :) The function f(n) = :(g(n))
